content:
4 min read
Just now

--

For one slightly ridiculous week, I tried to run my entire job through AI. Every email, every decision, every doc. By Thursday I’d saved real hours on some things and quietly created more work for myself on others, because I’d handed the tool tasks it had no business touching. That week was the most useful thing I’ve done to understand where AI belongs in a product manager’s work.
Press enter or click to view image in full size

The argument is simple: AI earns its place on the parts of your week that are drafting and synthesis, and it wastes your time on the parts that are judgment and stakes. The whole skill is knowing which is which before you reach for it.
Start with where it genuinely helps, because it helps a lot. Synthesis is the standout. I had two hundred pieces of customer feedback from a survey, the kind of pile I’d normally skim, half-read, and summarize badly at 6pm. Handed to AI with a clear brief, it clustered the mess into themes in minutes, and I spent my energy on the more valuable job of deciding what the themes meant. Same story with long email threads, messy meeting transcripts, and research notes. Anything where the work is “compress this sprawl into something I can think about” is a real win.
The other clear win is the blank page. A first draft of a PRD, a stakeholder update, a set of interview questions, a tricky message I’m avoiding. The draft it gives me is rarely what I send. It’s something to react to, and reacting is ten times faster than starting from nothing. As a proudly no-code PM, this is the part of the week where AI feels less like a tool and more like a fast, tireless junior who never minds being edited.
Now where it adds noise, which is the part people gloss over. Prioritization. I’ve tried asking AI to help decide what to build next, and what comes back is a confident, reasonable-sounding ranking that’s completely detached from the twenty things only I know: which stakeholder is quietly furious, which engineer is stretched thin, which “small” feature secretly touches everything. Prioritization is a judgment call soaked in context the model doesn’t have, and dressing it up in a tidy AI answer just adds a layer of false confidence I then have to argue myself out of.
It also adds noise anywhere the cost of being confidently wrong is high and the error is hard to spot. Handing a customer a number it invented. Trusting its read on why a metric moved. Letting it write the sensitive part of a message where tone is the whole point. And there’s a subtler waste: over-polishing. It’s easy to spend twenty minutes perfecting a doc with AI that only needed five minutes and a human sense of “good enough.” The tool makes more feel free, so you overproduce.
The pattern underneath all of this is one question. Am I about to outsource typing, or outsource thinking? Outsourcing typing, the drafting, the summarizing, the reformatting, is a gift, and you should take it freely. Outsourcing thinking, the deciding, the weighing, the reading of people, is a trap, because the thinking is the actual job and the model can’t hold your context. When I keep that line clear, AI gives me back hours. When I blur it, it quietly costs me.
One honest caveat, because I don’t want this to read as a fixed rulebook. The line moves. The tools keep getting better, and things that were firmly in the “adds noise” column two years ago are creeping toward useful, especially anything where the model can now look up real information instead of guessing. So don’t turn a bad experience into a permanent ban. Re-test occasionally. The skill isn’t memorizing what AI can’t do, it’s re-checking the boundary now and then and adjusting where you draw it.
So before you reach for AI this week, run the one test. If the task is getting words onto a page or getting signal out of a mess, let the machine do the heavy lifting and stay the editor. If the task is deciding what actually matters, do that part yourself, with the tool closed. Protect your judgment, delegate your typing, and AI stops being a shiny distraction and becomes the thing that gives you your Thursdays back.
